title: Does a VPN Slow Down Your Internet Speed? The Real Numbers
description: Myth vs fact: testing whether a VPN actually slows your connection and what causes performance loss.
content: The short answer is yes, a VPN typically slows your internet, but the degree depends entirely on the VPN provider and your location. A good VPN slows you by 10-20 percent. A bad VPN slows you by 80 percent. Understanding why this happens helps you choose a VPN that does not waste your bandwidth.

Your internet normally goes from your computer directly to your ISP and then to the website you are visiting. With a VPN, your traffic is encrypted and routed through a VPN server in another location, then to the destination. That extra hop creates latency and uses CPU resources to encrypt and decrypt data. The result: some slowdown is inevitable.

The amount of slowdown depends on four factors. First, the distance from you to the VPN server. If you are in New York and connect to a VPN server in New York, the slowdown is minimal. If you connect to a server in Tokyo, the slowdown is larger because the data travels farther. Second, the VPN's server load. If a VPN provider puts 10,000 users on one server, everyone shares that server's bandwidth and the slowdown is severe. Third, the VPN's encryption level. Military-grade AES-256 encryption requires more CPU than lighter algorithms, causing slightly more overhead. Fourth, your internet connection quality. If your baseline connection is fast, a 15 percent slowdown is unnoticeable. If your baseline is slow, 15 percent slowdown is painful.

Real-world testing shows the variation. ProtonVPN slows most users by 10-15 percent. ExpressVPN slows by 10-20 percent. NordVPN slows by 15-30 percent depending on the protocol chosen. Surfshark slows by 10-25 percent. Budget VPNs slow by 40-80 percent because they use cheap servers with high user density. This is why price correlates with speed: you pay for infrastructure that does not bottleneck you.

Streaming is the test that reveals slowdown most clearly. If your baseline connection is 100 Mbps, you need 25 Mbps to stream 4K Netflix without buffering. With a 15 percent VPN slowdown, you have 85 Mbps available. You still stream fine. But if your baseline is 30 Mbps and the VPN slows you 40 percent, you have 18 Mbps left, and 4K streaming becomes impossible. Gaming is similar: a 15 percent slowdown does not matter for turn-based games, but for online shooters where milliseconds count, a slowdown from 50 ms to 60 ms latency is noticeable.

The slowdown is also not linear. If a VPN slows you 15 percent at 100 Mbps, it does not necessarily slow you 15 percent at 300 Mbps. Some VPN providers use hardware optimizations that reduce overhead at higher speeds. This is another reason premium VPNs are faster: they invest in better hardware.

You can reduce slowdown by changing which server you connect to. Connecting to a server geographically close to you reduces latency. Connecting during off-peak hours reduces server congestion. Switching between encryption protocols (WireGuard is faster than OpenVPN) reduces CPU overhead. A good VPN gives you these choices. A bad VPN locks you into one server and protocol, so you are stuck with slowdown.

One common misconception: unlimited VPNs are slower than limited ones. This is backwards. The limitation of data is arbitrary and not related to speed. A VPN that caps you at 10 GB per month uses the same infrastructure as one offering unlimited data. The VPN that limits data is usually just extracting more money from users who hit the limit, not providing a faster network. Do not confuse limits with speed.

Another misconception: changing your IP address slows you down. It does not. The slowdown comes from the routing and encryption, not from spoofing your IP. A fast VPN with good servers feels just as fast as your regular connection, minus the encryption overhead, which is small.

The fix for slowdown is simple: choose a VPN with modern infrastructure and test it. ProtonVPN, Mullvad, and IVPN are benchmarked by multiple independent reviewers and consistently ranked as fastest. ExpressVPN is fast but expensive. NordVPN is slightly slower but good value. Budget VPNs are cheap for a reason: they are slow. Test your own connection before and after using your VPN's free trial. If you lose more than 30 percent of your speed, switch providers. A slowdown that large means the VPN is not optimized for your location or connection type.

Summary: a VPN always slows you somewhat, but a good VPN slows you by less than the loss of privacy you avoid. That is the trade you are making. If your baseline is 100 Mbps and you are willing to accept 85 Mbps in exchange for encryption and location spoofing, a premium VPN is worth it. If your baseline is 20 Mbps and the VPN slows you to 12 Mbps, the slowdown is painful and probably not worth it. Choose a VPN based on your baseline speed and what you can afford to lose.
